title: The One-Off Discount That Quietly Became Your New Price
linkedin:
A customer asks for a small discount. You say yes, meant as a one-off. Eighteen months later, it's simply the price.

We see this pattern constantly across owner-led businesses, and it rarely gets named because no single decision ever caused it.

Here's the mechanism. Once a customer receives a lower rate, that rate becomes their reference point. The next invoice at the original price doesn't read as normal to them, it reads as an increase, even though nothing has actually gone up. So the concession rolls forward quietly, invoice after invoice, without anyone deciding it should.

Nobody chooses to discount a valued customer forever. Undoing it feels like taking something away, so the conversation to reverse it keeps getting avoided. That's the whole mechanism. Not weak pricing discipline, just the absence of a review point.

The way to actually see it is to compare list price against realised price, customer by customer, rather than looking at revenue and gross margin as one combined number. It's common to find your highest-revenue customer carrying one of the thinnest margins in the business, purely from concessions accumulated over a long relationship rather than anything about the work itself.

The fix isn't refusing every request for flexibility. It's giving every concession a date it ends and a reason attached to it, so nothing becomes permanent purely by default.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#ProfitabilityAdvice #SMEFinance #BusinessOwners
facebook:
Ever given a customer "just this once" break on price, and a year later realised it's simply what they pay now?

It's one of the quieter ways margin slips in owner-led businesses. Nobody sits down and decides to discount a valued customer forever, it just never comes back up for review. Undoing it feels like taking something away, so the old price never gets reinstated.

The businesses that stay ahead of this compare what customers are actually paying against list price, account by account, rather than just watching total revenue.

Any concessions running on autopilot in your customer base that haven't been looked at in a while?

#SMEFinance
google:
A note for East Coast business owners: if a long-standing customer still pays a price you offered them as a one-off favour months or years ago, it's worth taking a second look at whether that concession is still doing its job.

Discounts given for genuine reasons, loyalty, a service issue, a tough quarter for a good customer, tend to become permanent pricing by default rather than by decision. Once a customer receives a lower rate, the next invoice at the original price reads as an increase to them, even though nothing has actually changed. So the lower rate quietly rolls forward, and most owners never consciously chose to keep it running.

The businesses that stay on top of this compare list price against what customers are actually paying, account by account, rather than watching revenue and margin as one combined number. It is common to find that a long-standing, high-revenue customer is quietly one of the least profitable accounts on the books, purely because of concessions nobody has revisited in years.

None of this means renegotiating every account overnight. It means giving every concession a reason and a date it ends, so pricing is a decision again rather than something that drifts on its own.

Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
